> Because virtual, it's no longer true the type of this is OneOf<T, Output>, it can be something else, which is taken advantage of in InputList

Right.

> I think it might be worth annotating this prop with a note

Yes, sounds good!

> I also wonder what about deserializer. The hook can break symmetry e.g. can InputMap deserialize from what it serializes to? Or are we riding on the understanding that InputMaps never deserialize?

This is a good question. I don't think the InputMap is going to show up during deserialization. The deserializer deserializes protobuf structs as `ImmutableDictionary<string, object?>`:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/f98d39b84bc0e3a78a13dc09f80730e39a297b10/sdk/dotnet/Pulumi/Serialization/Deserializer.cs#L75-L100

Typically, when you have an input typed as `InputMap<string>` the output will be `Output<ImmutableDictionary<string, string>>`.

> Where you might be doing this is for example in writing typed provider mocks (not a feature yet)

Hmmm. This is a good question. This shouldn't be a problem with our current `IMocks` abstraction, but typed provider mocks is interesting to think about. This will also likely come up when we get around to implementing nice provider APIs for .NET (for multi-lang components) where we'll want to take serialized inputs and deserialize them into the inputs that a component expects, which could be `InputList<T>` or `InputMap<V>`. Let me think about this some more. I suspect we'll be able to make this work without too much trouble.

Please note that `type.__getitem__` is a [Python 3.9+ functionality](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#generic-alias-type).
This should still not cause trouble at runtime for 3.7/3.8 due to the [postponed evaluation](https://peps.python.org/pep-0563/) brought in via `from __future__ import annotations`.

However, beware that using features/properties of later versions can give a false sense of confirmation from a type checker. Usually it's good to also run a type checker for the lowest supported version.
(^-- That is a very general note. In this certain case it's not a big deal, probably. But if/when we have type checking as part of the CI, it will likely complain.)

As far as I understand, there are two main reasons for `Crud` class:
- to store `_crud_module_init_complete` variable,
- to be able to use module as a namespace of `conn`: `conn.crud`, since you'll need conn anyway.

I think it is possible to get rid of both of them.

Above I've discussed that there are seems to be no need to call `crud = require('crud')`. You may check that the instance is a crud router (we may call any operations on routers only, we have no interest in interacting with storages explicitly) by `rawget(_G, 'crud') != nil`. But do we really need that? It seems like an optimization, but it is rather hard to have our "cache" consistent: if one would disable crud router (highly unlikely, but not impossible), it will show that require is ok which is untrue. And the main point is we actually don't need to know if we're able to call crud procedures: if we're don't, we will get "Procedure not defined" (code 33) error. It is rather simple to make this error more useful: just wrap each code 33 error with comment "Ensure that you're calling crud.router and user have enough grants". It seems no use to fast fail since if user would try to call `crud` thousands of times from a connection to an instance without `crud` they are doing something really weird and it's not worth to optimize it.

Why don't we do the entire set of chained operations up until some threshold (e.g. 80 characters), and if it's over that threshold, we slice off the (post dataset name) operation prefix until we're under that threshold? Should be some simple enough string manipulation and gives us the most informative task name without letting it get too long.
```
# Under threshold.
ray::MyDataset-map-filter-random_shuffle()
# Over threshold.
ray::MyDataset-...-filter-random_shuffle()
```
```python
OP_SEPARATOR = "-"
TASK_NAME_LENGTH_LIMIT = 75  # 80 - len("ray::")

def _get_task_name(self, op_name: str):
    name = self._get_name() + OP_SEPARATOR + op_name
    if len(name) < TASK_NAME_LENGTH_LIMIT:
        return name
    splits = name.split(OP_SEPARATOR)
    filler = "..."
    running_len = len(splits[0]) + len(filler) + 1
    out = []
    for split in splits[:0:-1]:
        running_len += len(split) + 1
        if running_len > TASK_NAME_LENGTH_LIMIT:
            break
        out.append(split)
    out.append(filler)
    out.append(splits[0])
    return OP_SEPARATOR.join(list(reversed(out))
```
This would then be easy to tweak/extend in the future, e.g. maybe we change the `filler` from an ellipsis to a chain of the first letter of each dropped op, `ray::MyDataset-f-m-m-m-random_shuffle()`.

Leaving `NpxToolBase` for now. Down the road when we think about potentially incorporating `yarn`, `pnpm`, or whatever the flavour of the month is in the future - we can revisit. 

@benjyw `npx` isn't a package manager per se, but (more or less) an alias for "npm install" and then "npm exec" (https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v7/commands/npx#npx-vs-npm-exec). So, `npm` is the `pip`.

This line of thinking has gotten me to the conclusion that `NpxToolBase` is better for now, specifically because it brings with it one piece of implicit info. The package MUST be executable with a `bin` folder/file specified in the `package.json`. 

For example: `npx dayjs` errors out with `npm ERR! could not determine executable to run`. 

So, `NpmToolBase` is incorrect, because the intention is an executable package. And for the same reason, I think `NodeToolBase` is incorrect, as you can put in Node packages which aren't executable, and then this `ToolBase` would be useless (without adding some extra flags, options, and shenanigans).

I think the current logic is correct because of 2 reasons:
1) I think we should have very strong distinction and separation between Internal and External messages. I think from the business logic point of view it should have no (or almost no) difference. Think about a service as an Actor receiving messages. It doesn't matter how the message was delivered. Moreover, depending on the configuration of actors/services, we may change message delivery (using of internal/external/inter process buses). 
2) I suspect that if we have different stashers for internal and external messages, then we may break the order of received messages (which is critical for unstash) and have the same  we had situation we had with different stasher instances created for Checkpointer and Ordering Services.

So it looks like `LeagueEntry`-like objects (which I'm defining as objects that contain information about the league of a specific summoner) never have the `leagueName` field, which you pointed out. Instead, the league name is only available when requesting a league via it's ID, and (maybe coincidentally) when requesting challenger/grandmaster/master.

Because of that, my gut reaction is that the League should be treated like the Summoner for these LeagueEntry objects. I.e. each LeagueEntry corresponds to a summoner, so it has a `.summoner` attribute that returns a Summoner object; and similarly, each LeagueEntry corresponds to a league, so it has a `.league` attribute that returns a League object. There is a difference: the LeagueEntry and Summoner objects are (for a give queue) one-to-one, whereas the LeagueEntry and League objects are many-to-one. I think that's fine though. It's a little weird that you'd be able to access the "parent" object of a LeagueEntry by hitting the `.league` attribute, however. I'm not sure I like that.

I'm a bit up in the air right now about this. It seems like there are two options:

Option 1: Each `LeagueEntry` has a `.league_name` and `.league_id` attribute.

Option 2: Each `LeagueEntry` has a `.league` attribute which in turn has a `.name` and `.id` attribute.

Option 2 seems more "correct" because the `.name` and `.id` are on the correct object (the `League` rather than the `LeagueEntry`). On the other hand, it's a bit strange to be accessing the "parent" object from the child object since, technically, every `LeagueEntry` belongs to a given `League`.

Wherever you install or list `pybind11` as a package, you can list `pybind11[global]` instead (though you need quotes on the command line if you are in cmd.exe). So in `setup.py`, `requirements.txt`, `Pipfile`, `pyproject.toml`, etc.

`pybind11` only places files in `<ENVROOT>/lib/pythonX.X/site-packages/pybind11`. `include` and `share` are added to the pybind11 folder. (This was done in pybind11 2.4 or 2.5, and solved a lot of search issues)

`pybind11-global` only places includes and cmake files in  (no python files) in `<ENVROOT>/include` and `<ENVROOT>/share`. If `<ENVROOT>` is your system root, this is a little ugly. However, for pyproject.toml, this is always a venv, and it's fine in venvs, conda, and anywhere that is not your system root. (This is manually hacked in by the conda-forge recipe currently)

> Mostly for backward compatibility, `pybind11-global` also puts a copy of the pybind11 headers in the official "headers" slot, something like `include/pythonX.X<m>/`, but this is very hard to even find (it's the reason behind the old "user=True/False` setting, since it's not the same for a user install vs. a normal install). This was the only place they were put pre 2.4 or 2.5.

Flask store one cookie:
```
curl -v --cookie "USER_ATTACK=abc;USER_ATTACK=dfg" http://localhost:5000/

@app.route('/')
def check_cookie():
    print(request.cookies["USER_ATTACK"])
>> 'abc'

Flask stores this data in InmutableMultiDict but this class changes the behavior between versions:

Flask 0.12:
```
dict(request.cookies)
>> {'USER_ATTACK': [u"1' or '1' = '1'", u'blablabla']}
dict(request.cookies["USER_ATTACK"])
>> 'abc'
```

Flask >0.12:
```
dict(request.cookies)
>> {'USER_ATTACK': "1' or '1' = '1'"}
dict(request.cookies["USER_ATTACK"])
>> 'abc'
```

> Na it's not cmake. It's easy to have real clean cmake. It's all complicated because there are so many people with broken systems or compilers or whatever. You can make it so simple. But then someone posts "hey, I'm building on $some_weird_linux_distro_or_whatever and I'm getting this linker error saying libpng is missing some symbol". Or they are complaining about something else that is actually broken on their system. For instance, Anaconda includes these copies of x11 header files right, which for a lot of people end up in their compiler's include search path. And Anaconda has helpfully edited those headers to be different from the real x11 headers, so if those get included instead the world breaks. I could go on and on.
> 
> Anyway, those people complain to me though. "Hey, dlib isn't building?!". So I add some logic to the dlib cmake scripts to detect whatever is wrong with their system and work around it. Like all that logic in there for testing if libpng is _really_ there or not.
> 
> The only way this whole mess could be resolved was if applications _never ever_ depended on libraries they are not themselves building. And the building of those libraries would have to be controlled by one unified build system. But that's a tall ask because everyone then turns around and says "hey, I hate the build times. Can't the $(os, package system, whatever) provide precompiled binaries instead?" which is a desire 100% contradictory to resolving this problem given the nature of the One Definition Rule and the diversity of compile and build settings.
> 
> Then you top this all off with proprietary vendor libraries. Like IntelMKL or anything CUDA related. Which you can't build. They have to be somehow pulled in as binaries because that's the only things those vendors will give out. And those binaries have OS dependencies and even driver level dependencies.
> 
> So IDK. Any language that wants to do anything with hardware acceleration has to grapple with this. I guess in the dream world there would be BLAS, LAPACK, and CUDA built into compilers by default. > Why is such a partitioning scheme better than the default one?

What do you mean by 'default'? 

If 'default' is the current behavior then the answer would be is that it benefits from knowing in advance an approximate shape of the result. By default, the number of splits across an axis is equal to `NPartitions` (let's say it's 112 for example :D), meaning that it's highly likely that there'll be A LOT of empty partitions that will then slow down overall performance.

> Is it suitable only for merge operation?

Yes. `.merge` and `.join` to be precise. We don't have other QC methods that insert 2D frames bypassing `modin_frame.concat`. There are also `.wrtie_items` and `.setitems` but they seem to not change the shape of the frame.

> Is it beneficial to just make this behavior the default? (when num_splits=auto)

Inside `.apply_full_axis` we don't know the shape of `other` frame we're inserting into the source one.

**TL;DR:** How about we have a single `shuffle_buffer_min_size` argument for now, and introduce the planned `buffer_capacity` argument (which would replace `prefetch_blocks` and apply to both shuffling and non-shuffling batchers) in one of the follow-up PRs?

## Context

It's advantageous to have these as separate arguments because they control different things.
1. `shuffle_buffer_min_size` controls the minimum size of the buffer before random batches can be extracted; **this controls the randomness of the shuffle**.
2. `shuffle_buffer_capacity` controls the maximum size that the buffer can achieve before we start applying backpressure to the producer (e.g. stalling prefetching); **this controls the memory size limit for the shuffle buffer**.

Increasing (1) improves shuffle quality at the cost of longer latency-to-batch, while increasing (2) improves throughput at the cost of higher memory utilization; e.g. you could imagine keeping `shuffle_buffer_min_size` fixed at 50k samples since that randomness suffices, while increasing `shuffle_buffer_capacity` to 250k samples in order to get higher throughput via more aggressive prefetching, up to the CPU memory limit of the trainer. This is why these are exposed as separate knobs in TensorFlow Datasets via a prefetching buffer size and shuffling buffer size (see [SO answer](https://stackoverflow.com/a/47025850)) and in the [Petastorm Torch dataloader](https://petastorm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#petastorm.spark.spark_dataset_converter.SparkDatasetConverter.make_torch_dataloader) (although Petastorm only exposes a `shuffle_queue_capacity` and sets `shuffle_buffer_min_size = shuffle_queue_capacity - 1` and `shuffle_buffer_capacity = shuffle_buffer_min_size + batch_size`).

However, for this first PR, `shuffle_buffer_capacity` doesn't do much because prefetching is still controlled by `prefetch_blocks` and prefetching + shuffle-batching is synchronous. The eventual plan is to consolidate `shuffle_buffer_capacity` and `prefetch_blocks` into a single `buffer_capacity` argument (used for both the shuffling batcher and the regular batcher), where we aggressively prefetch blocks until the `buffer_capacity` is hit, at which point backpressure is applied; the plan is to do this in a follow up PR to this first shuffle PR, once we start moving shuffled and non-shuffled batch into the background, where prefetching already operates.

How about we have a single `shuffle_buffer_min_size` argument for now, and introduce the planned `buffer_capacity` argument (which would replace `prefetch_blocks` and apply to both shuffling and non-shuffling batchers) in one of the follow-up PRs?

Not exactly. By default, west uses `git checkout --detach` to update each project to the new manifest revision. This does not affect any uncommitted working tree changes as long as they don't conflict with the checked out revision.

It does leave behind any commits that aren't part of the new manifest-rev unless you use `-r`. It leaves a detached HEAD unless you use `-r` or sometimes when you use `-k`.

I agree with @marc-hb that "west with submodules" should try to at least keep the default behavior:

1. leave a detached HEAD checked out
2. leave any uncommitted working tree changes alone
3. error out if uncommited changes conflict with the new submodule revision

Is that possible to do with git submodules?

> I think yes, recursive by default. However I've used west imports only once for pure testing purposes so far. So for that and other reasons please wait for @mbolivar-nordic

I think recursing into submodules of submodules is a good idea if it is possible / practical to get "everything" by default.

We can always add additional configuration to do more complicated stuff:

```yaml
manifest:
  projects:
  - name: foo
    submodules:
      recurse-behavior: whatever
```

Note I am **not** suggesting we add a `recurse-behavior` key right now. I'm just saying we could do it later if we need finer grained control.

But I am not a submodules user (hey, I just use west...) so I don't know if that addresses people's use cases and I want the users to chime in here. Thanks.

Sorry, I wrote that very unclearly.  Yes, the inverse fft is going to have complex inputs.  But the forward fft can take real inputs just fine.  There isn't any need to make the user write `ffti(whatever)` when we know the type of `whatever`.  There shouldn't be anything to check, as we do the checking by having the appropriate overload.

Then `ifft()` is always a function that maps from a complex input to a complex output.  Someone can just do ```
matrix<double> m = real(ifft(some_complex_matrix));
```
if they want just the real part.  It's possible to make `real(ifft(some_complex_matrix))` map to a different inverse fft function than just `ifft(some_complex_matrix)`.  But it is simpler to write `ifftr` and just document that it's logically equivalent to `real(ifft())`, so it's got that going for it and is maybe fine.  But `fftr` has no such complications.

The issue with the matcher setup is that the test proxy is failing to process multiple matcher requests in quick succession. As part of https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/23148, I had to add a `set_custom_default_matcher` call to `proxy_startup.py`, which is run during test session startup, in order to make tests backwards-compatible after removing some headers from recordings. What's happening here is that these tests are making another request to set a matcher right after `proxy_startup.py` is run, and the test proxy can't handle that request yet.

The solution will require either waiting to make a matcher set request until the proxy is ready to handle it, or updating old recordings so that we don't need the test proxy tooling to be backwards-compatible and set this initial matcher. That's partially correct. We will delete all other kvs **with specified prefix and label** except newly imported ones.
i.e.

Consider the following kvs are present in the config store - 

| Key | Label | Value |
|----|----|----|
| TestApp:BackgroundColor | label1 | Blue |
| TestApp:BackgroundColor | label2 | Red |
| TestApp:Font | label1 | 50 |
| OtherKey | None | OtherValue |

And the file contains following keyvalues - 
| Key | Label | Value |
|----|----|----|
| TestApp:BackgroundColor | label1 | Green |

When user imports using `strict` mode, and specifies the label `label1` and prefix `TestApp`, then 
following actions will happen - 
1. The value for TestApp:BackgroundColor with label1 will be updated to `Green`.
1. The key TestApp:Font with `label1` will be deleted.
1. Other key values will remain unchanged.

The final result in the configuration store would be - 
| Key | Label | Value |
|----|----|----|
| TestApp:BackgroundColor | label1 | Green |
| TestApp:BackgroundColor | label2 | Red |
| OtherKey | None | OtherValue |

I'm not really worried about performance. I'm worried about two things: the likelihood of running into bugs (security or correctness) in the insertion of arguments into a `text()` representation of the query, and the loss of flexibility with the generated query. When we return `Selectable`, the user is able to take that and use it in many different ways: add clauses (e.g. `LIMIT` or `ORDER BY`), plug it in elsewhere without immediately executing the query (e.g. use it to create a new vertex backed by the `Selectable`) etc.

Not having two different execution paths certainly simplifies _our_ lives as maintainers. But I'm not convinced it's the right thing to do from a user-facing perspective. So I'm inclined to leave the flexibility in and take the slight maintainability hit here.

The function lookup cost isn't assessed in this equation -- a function lookup is constant (since functions are only defined in the top-level scope of a contract -- so a single hashmap lookup) or in the case of a contract-call, includes the cost of loading a contract.

(a*X+b) is the cost of type checking supplied arguments -- type checks are linear with respect to the size of their describes objects (e.g., type-checking a `(list int 5)` is `(a*5+b)`). The process for a function application is:

1. Type check arguments.
2. Bind the arguments to a new local scope (this used to be described as variable in argument names, but each binding is constant because variable names are bounded).
3. Evaluate the function body.

If you only specify the name `mxnetci/dockcross-linux-armv7-pinned`, instead of `mxnetci/dockcross-linux-armv7-pinned:TAG`, docker will always pull the version tagged as `latest`: `mxnetci/dockcross-linux-armv7-pinned:latest`.
But that version changes whenever someone pushes a new version of `dockcross-linux-armv7-pinned` to `mxnetci`. So it becomes impossible to test if an update of `dockcross-linux-armv7-pinned` breaks the CI. Instead all CI runs will be changed immediately.

If instead you add a tag `TAG` for the particular version of the `mxnetci/dockcross-linux-armv7-pinned` container at https://hub.docker.com/r/mxnetci/dockcross-linux-armv7-pinned/tags, our Dockerfiles here on Github can mention the tag `mxnetci/dockcross-linux-armv7-pinned:TAG`. Then, when pushing a new update of `dockcross-linux-armv7-pinned` to `mxnetci` and tagged as `TAG2`, the CI will continue to use the old version until a PR is opened to update to use `TAG2` on github.

Not sure if I have the right solution for you, but a couple of thoughts:

1. I think it is important to expose this behavior explicitly to user. While doing this implicitly behind the scenes is convenient, it make the process intransparent.
1. We should try not to mix front-end and backend functionality, i.e, using the HDF5-specific H5DataIO in the ObjectMapper (or Container) is problematic as this will not translate to other backends.
1. This issue also has come up with DynamicTable at some point, because we wanted all columns of the table to be chunked so they can be extended. @rly @ajtritt was that issue solved and would that same strategy apply here?

> 
> The old behavior: if last successful credential fails, we try next
> New behavior: if last successful credential fails, we return fail.

We must be more specific here. It depends on how the credential fails:
- `CredentialUnavailableError`: try the next credential
- `ClientAuthenticationError`: raise

Credentials generally don't raise `CredentialUnavailableError` after having succeeded, which means "old" and "new" behavior are equivalent in most cases. `AzureCliCredential` could do it, if someone uninstalled the CLI or the shell between calls to `get_token`. However, that credential is at the end of the chain unless the user opted in to interactive authentication, so the behavior is still typically unchanged.

Back to your question:
> Is this expected?

Yes. Consider the alternative: how surprised would you be if `DefaultAzureCredential` authenticated with the CLI the first time, then popped a browser for you to log in the second time?

>I'm not sure the built-in Queue has the right semantics for the use here -- that is, that Queue is more-or-less designed around the notion of each item in the queue being handled discretely from the others, whereas in our use we want to treat everything currently in the queue as a single block of data to reduce the number of separate requests that are sent to our servers. Obviously you could implement such semantics using the Queue but it's not quite as simple as saying "let's just use a queue". In some ways it might be quite clunky to do, in fact, since the Queue would not prohibit threads adding to the queue at just the moment the flushing thread was trying to pull things out to buffer for transmission. Again, not an unsolvable problem but not an obvious slam dunk replacement either, imo.

>Also a minor semantic nit: it's misleading to suggest that using Queue would remove locking altogether. The locking is still there, it's just pushed into the Queue implementation. That's easier for our application, to be sure, but does come with caveats -- for example, we'd no longer be able to lock around multiple actions at once, as we do now. I don't know whether we really need to do that, but it appears that we do do that now, so we'd need to be mindful of the change.

>I'm not familiar with the specifics of how the library maintainers have chosen to implement locking in that component, so I can't really speak to whether the performance characteristics and other behaviors of their choices are suitable for our needs here.

Right. My thought would be that we do something like the following at the time we send data:
```
# Get the size of the queue. Since there is only ever one thread consuming from it this is guaranteed to only either stay the same size or increase during the time this thread is executing
curr_queue_size = self.queue.qsize()

# Consume elements from queue
logs_to_send = [self.queue.get(block=False) for _ in range(curr_queue_size)]

# Continue the flush per existing semantics
self.send_request(logs_to_send)
```

That being said, I see your point and can see how the Queue implementation could possibly yield worse performance due to context switches between threads while handling multiple concurrent calls to `add()` and `get()`. The current implementation has the advantage that the thing that does the draining can consume all of the buffered data in a single locked section.

I think it would be worth to mention that this method is called when something like `bool(cluster.failover)` or `if cluster.failover` is executed.

The reason it was done this way because Patroni need to create/update/delete failover key atomically, but ZooKeeper implements atomic operations it very weirdly. In ZooKeeper these operations are based on version of a given ZNode, which is reset to 0 every time when ZNode is dropper and created. That is, we have a /failover key with version=0 and want to remove it, but meanwhile some other thread removed and added it back. As a result we will be removing not a key we wanted to remove.

To mitigate this behavior Patroni just empties the /failover key instead of removing it. When we read the cluster state from DCS, the Failover object will be created, because we need to know its version for the next update.

Without `__len__` we would have to write something like `if cluster.failover and (cluster.failover.leader or cluster.failover.candidate)` most of the time, and now we can just do `if cluster.failover`, which will be evaluated to `False` if object is "empty".

Since it's all going to be contained in this one file, we don't need a `kernels` directory. As part of the C++ library, the file should be named after the one class it contains. Also because there's only one class, the word "Core" is unnecessary.

So how about files named include/awkward/Kernel.h and src/libawkward/Kernel.cpp defining a class named Kernel?

We should keep in mind that this class is part of the C++ libawkward.so, not the C libawkward-cpu-kernels.so or libawkward-gpu-kernels. One of the features it provides is re-templatizing C functions named

   * `awkward_listarray32_getitem_XYZ`
   * `awkward_listarrayU32_getitem_XYZ`
   * `awkward_listarray64_getitem_XYZ`

into a single static method named

   * `Kernel::listarray_getitem_XYZ<T>`

which is something that all of those free functions in `util::` are currently doing (without removing the `awkward_` prefix, but the `Kernel::` class lets us do that).

Also worth considering: if `Kernel` only contains static methods, then it can be a namespace, `kernel::` (lowerspace because it's not a class, and the file name would follow suit). What do you think of that? Do you see any advantages/disadvantages of it being a class?

In fact your question is closely related to the other one about absolute imports and the fact that absolute imports are not tied to an actual filesystem architecture. `pkgutil.iter_modules` does not care about the Python files, it cares about what is resolved as packages and modules in the current Python runtime.

Namely, it is: 'iterate over all the modules available in the given package'. In that extend, `__init__` does not exists. It is just a file containing the logic to construct the relevant package, but it is not itself a module. So it does not appear in the iteration.

What are the other advantages ? Well, imagine that something (certainly `__init__.py`) construct and dynamically add a module in a package. If you rely on the filesystem, you will not see the dynamic module, because there is not file for it. But if you call `pkgutil.iter_modules`, you will see it.

Yes, this will return an empty `continuation` as it does today. I'm not sure the worst-case blocking time here but it could theoretically get pretty large if the directory is huge. Currently customers that call this API today would also be blocked on the single call as the service would not paginate and I'm not sure we get many/any complaints on that. I also don't anticipate many customers will actually run into this scenario as you need to be using OAuth with file level ACLs rather than RBAC permissions to the Storage account for this to paginate. I don't think this is super common workflow, at least for REST API usage (maybe SMB, NFS, etc.). One other thing to note is that the continuation token only lasts for 4 minutes so saving it and passing it in later doesn't make too much sense as it would likely be expired by the time you try and start again.

So ultimately my opinion is that we don't need to add any special stuff here unless we get feedback from customers in this area. Timeouts are probably things we can always add later if needed.

My position regarding locking and concurrency is to always to exactly what's necessary, nothing more or less. Doing less is self explanatory, but doing unnecessary locking makes things (IMO) more confusing.

e.g. in this case if you have a lock for both _val and _count, then you can infer that they should be updated or acquired together -- they're treated as one atomic value. If you then start locking them individually as well, I'm wondering... are they a single atomic value or not? In what circumstances would you lock and update one and not the other?

SumAggregationDataInt = functools.partial(SumAggregationData, value_type=value.ValueInt)
```

But then in `SumAggregation.new_aggregation_data`, we needed an additional set of conditionals to check the type of the measure or metric and decide which to use. Instead doing it this way, we just need the one set of conditionals in .`get_metric_type` which we need anyway, and can reuse `MetricDescriptor.to_type_class`. It seemed a little cleaner, but really it isn't that different.

We could also duplicate the classes entirely, instead of the `functools.partial`, but that seemed unnecessary.

I guess the other option is to force the user to decide and provide Int or Float, but since we can determine it that seems easier and less error prone.

std::string name = "....";
GrpcServer(std::move(name));  // no copy at all.

> So height or intensity of the ellipsoid mean the same?
Yes, I should be consistent with this. Which of the two terms is more intuitive?

>   For those x, y inside the ellipsoid, this corresponds to `intensity_vertex * np.sqrt(np.clip(1 - tmp, 0, None))` right?
Yes.

> * We are interested in the maximal achievable height of this ellipsoid while it is still covered by the image's "intensity stacks", right? 
Exactly.

>   Why the `+ c`? Shouldn't it be `- c` if the background corresponds to the bottom of the ellipsoid or "`+ 0`" if the background corresponds to the center of the ellipsoid? Which of those is true?
Neither of the last two is true; the background is at the apex/top of the ellipsis. The way I think about it is the following: 

To estimate the intensity of the background for some pixel, we introduce a temporary coordinate system that has its origin at that pixel position (the window). In other words, we always estimate the intensity for position `(0,0)` (wlog. ;) ). In this temporary coordinate system, we can easily describe the neighborhood of the pixel as well as the ellipsoid.

If we simplify the algorithm and only consider a rolling disk (the approximation from #3538 ) for a second then the background is simply:
```
b = min_{x,y} w(x,y)
```
where `w(x,y)` is the intensity at that window position.

When we extend this to an ellipsoid (i.e., put a cap on top of the disk), we need to introduce a term that corrects/accounts for the cap. How much higher is the cap in the center (where we estimate the intensity) compared to the position `(x,y)` (where the ellipsoid touches)? At `(0,0)` the height is exactly `c`, at `(x, y)` the height is `z(x,y)`, and the difference `c - z(x.y)` is exactly the amount by which the center is higher than the position `(x,y)`. Hence the background intensity is
```
b = min_{x, y} w(x, y,) + (c - z(x, y))
```

If a malformed enh-ack is injected before a genuine enh-ack, the radio state machine will jump to some state like tx frame acked. So the following genuine enh-ack will just be ignored. Also, this case will not happen because ack sending does not go through CSMA/CA process. For example, A is the data frame transmitter, B is the intended genuine receiver, C is an intruder. When A sends out a data frame. B and C both receives this. B will send out genuine enh-ack after AIFS(192us) without doing any CCA. If C sends out a malformed ack, this ack will collide with B's ack and results in an NO_ACK for A.

The problem is that in this solution, we can only get one level `cause` of cross language exception in Java. Suppose there is an exception chain: `Java -> Python -> Java`, Python can only concat the string of inner Java exception, and the outer Java exception will get one level `cause`: the Python exception.

I will add more strict assertions, but the assertions are on the exception string not the `cause` exception object.

I think we still need a batch lock in stop() because commit() will race with batch creation & publish otherwise. Consider this ordering:

T1: client.publish() runs, sees is_stopped is False, and continues.
T2: sets is_stopped to True, runs commit() on all existing batches
T1: still in publish(), adds message to new/old batch and publishes. This causes a message addition to a committed batch (this results in batch.publish() returning a None future, causing a new batch to be created and not published immediately- a dangling batch), or starts a new batch that is then published (this new batch has to wait for the commit timer to commit - a dangling batch).

As I understand it (but correct me if I'm wrong!), the "MiB" notation explicitly means `1024` (`2**10`) multipliers, whereas for "MB" its can mean either. In practice almost everybody uses the 1024 multiplier, with the exception of hard drive manufacturers. So I'd prefer to always use the 1024 multiplier, and allow both the "MiB" and "MB" notation.

The issue is that a developer can supply any value to `--parallel`, and Django will create that many test databases up to a maximum of the number of TestCase classes that exist. I could create 1000 databases with `manage.py --parallel 1000 --keepdb`. The `drop_test_database` code has no knowledge of the value last used with `--parallel`, so some other strategy must exist to halt the loop that looks for test databases to delete. 

The most reasonable strategy to my mind is checking sequential existence, since the only guarantee we can rely on from Django's process is that the test database names are sequentially numbered. That does leave the caveat you point out that a developer could manually delete some test database in the middle and then run `drop_test_database`, but to me it seems that would be the developer's responsibility to clean up, since it's an odd thing to do.

Alternatively, an arbitrarily high upper limit on the possible number could be hard-coded into the loop, and the loop could always run that number of times, checking all database names. This approach to me is inferior, since it relies on guessing some value assumed to be higher than any developer would use, and is slower since it would need to run that number of database queries (or file existence checks in the case of sqlite) every time the command is run. And, despite the downsides, it still leaves its own caveat since some developer could always create a larger number of test database than the maximum we set on this loop and those won't get deleted.

If you do want the second strategy, let me know what value cap you want to use, and I'll implement that. Or if you have an idea for a different strategy to solve this problem, let me know. But my current recommendation would be what's implemented, and perhaps if you prefer, I could add something to the docs warning that the command may not remove everything if a developer manually deletes any test databases first.

> The fetch_schema attribute in the connection object should not be available to the user, it should be made private (now it is possible to change the parameter like conn.fetch_schema=... for an existing conn object, now fetch_schema is public)

There are no convenient in-built ways to declare a "private" in Python class: either you start it with underscore (and it still would be public) or mess with getters/setters overwriting, similar to Lua (and it's mostly don't worth it). You can leave it as is now, but never rely on someone would change that and do not teach others to do so through examples and tests.

> * If it is decided to make the `fetch_schema` private, then we can implement a getter (`conn.is_fetch_schema_enable() -> Bool`), so we can understand the type of connection.

It's also not worth it. I don't think that it be used that much often, and if one really needs it, he can just look up our "private" variable.



> * However, the connection can be made from `schema-less` to `schema-full`. To do this, it is enough for the user to call `conn.update_schema(conn.schema_version)`. After that, all methods that were previously unavailable for `schema-less` connection become available. At the same time, the scheme starts updating automatically. And `conn.is_fetch_schema_enable()` will start returning `True`.

> * It is not possible to change the connection type from `schema-full` to `schema-less`.

Yeah, I propose to use this approach.

This word-splitting is dangerous if there are ever spaces in the file
paths, which could occur if the user’s workspace has spaces in it (due
to `TEST_SRCDIR`). The unquoted expansion of `$DOCS_DIR` above is
likewise dangerous.

See <http://cl/276393831> for a recent occurrence of exactly this issue.

A safer way to do this is to save the output of `find` to a file and
iterate over its lines\* with `while read -r line`. (Usually you could
pipe the `find` directly into the `while`, but that creates a subshell,
and here you depend on modifications to `code` being propagated back
out.)

Or, see also suggestion below to consider using Python to avoid such
issues. :-)

\* This still assumes that no filename has *newlines* in it, but this is
generally a bit safer. If you want to be really robust you can use NUL
delimiters, which cannot appear in filenames.

Ok. Couple of thoughts here.

This has never worked up to this point, which means that no existing SAM templates will have a conditional inside of a method configuration. So, whatever we do here won't *break* any existing customers, but could potentially affect *future* uses of SAM.

SAM uses this code, I think, only for two things:
1. Creating Swagger for the user (generating APIGW paths and methods)
1. Adding an authorizer to a method.

This means that the only time (at least as SAM is now) a customer would have SAM read their swagger is when they are using custom authorizers. We should still assume that other use cases could arise.

Possible solutions:

1. Leave as is:
    1. Document that SAM only modifies the first element of a Condition that is nested under a method.
    1. This means that, with authorizers, SAM would only add an authorizer to the first item in the conditional and ignore the second entirely. 
    1. SAM will also throw an error if the first item is invalid.
1. Check the first item to see if it is a valid path, and, if not, return the second item.
    1. This is an either/or situation. It still wouldn't handle the case where both paths would need to be modified.
1. Just do whatever we're doing to both items in the list, and throw an error only if both of them are invalid paths.
    1. Might be the safest approach.  
    1. Would a customer ever want SAM to only modify the first one and not touch the second? 
    1. Or should we return both parts of this conditional list and make sure that it is a valid path before modifying it (for authorizers or other similar things we do in the future)?

@ssbarnea yes, that would be one subcase.
Although, I've been doing this in particular to avoid a slightly different race condition.
When the tags are pushed, this generates 2 workflows: for tag `push` and for tag `create`.
This means that whenever both of these workflows will run at about the same time and the checkout action will get the tag and the commits. And then, setuptools-scm will generate a version with that tag exactly. And this is fine because later in the workflow, the publish step is executed under only one of these cases so both jobs won't attempt to upload. That's why this step is skipped for these cases.
Now, there's a third job that may run separately in the context of the same commit: `push` to master. And if that commit is also tagged (for example, if the tagging happens fast after pushing the same commit to master, before some of the jobs did checkout), setuptools-scm will generate a version based on that tag. This is bad when that "Publish to test.pypi.org" step is uncommented because it'll result in two jobs attempting to publish an artifact with the same name to TestPyPI and one of them will fail (with no guarantee which one). And so for this case, I'm removing any tags that may be present in the HEAD which eliminates the race condition.

I'm a little bit uncertain about the behavior of `return_when="FIRST_EXCEPTION"`. From [the docs](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/asyncio-task.html#asyncio.wait):

> The function will return when any future finishes by raising an exception. If no future raises an exception then it is equivalent to ALL_COMPLETED.

If this stops at the very first exception--i.e. before giving whatever is waiting on the task a chance to handle the exception--then I don't think that we should cancel pending tasks, and should instead use `ALL_COMPLETED`.

For example, consider the following:

```python

async def raises():
    await asyncio.sleep(0)
    raise Exception("oh no")

async def catches():
    try:
        await raises()
    except Exception:
        print("oh yeah")

catches()
```

If `return_when="FIRST_EXCEPTION"` returns after `raises` raises its exception without allowing `catches` a chance to run, then we'll cancel `catches` and unintentionally abort a program that would not fail.

I was thinking a little about what a sensible default value of timeout would be here. Since that timeout applies to every individual network wait-for-reply, the max time the function takes is only capped at: `num_ip_addresses_in_network / max_threads * timeout`, so if the network is large, that time could be large and the real time it takes is at least proportional to the timeout. So the thing is that if we do get a reply, I would expect it to be fairly quick. On my own network this function return the correct result with timeout as low as 1E-5:
```
>>> scan_network(timeout=0.001)
{SoCo("192.168.1.173"), SoCo("192.168.1.197"), SoCo("192.168.1.31"), SoCo("192.168.1.18"), SoCo("192.168.1.215")}
>>> scan_network(timeout=1E-5)
{SoCo("192.168.1.173"), SoCo("192.168.1.197"), SoCo("192.168.1.31"), SoCo("192.168.1.18"), SoCo("192.168.1.215")}
>>> scan_network(timeout=1E-6)
>>>
```

One should of course always be careful with extrapolating from just one case, but setting it to say 0.1 would still be 1E4 more than my lowest observed success case. I know that you tested this on multiple different networks, so I would be interested to hear what your thoughts are.

I looked briefly at what we do in Java and .NET here (on both the question of how the empty string is used for a username and what happens in the case where there are multiple identities in the cache with the same username).

.NET:  In .NET, an [empty string is treated as the same as `null` username](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/master/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/src/SharedTokenCacheCredential.cs#L98-L103).  In this case, we .NET requires that a single identity be in the cache.  If a username is provided, the cache is filtered based on username, but [after filtering there must be a *single* account left](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/master/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/src/SharedTokenCacheCredential.cs#L106).

Java: In [Java](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-java/blob/master/sdk/identity/azure-identity/src/main/java/com/azure/identity/SharedTokenCacheCredential.java#L72-L97). an empty string for the username means "find the account in the cache with an empty username".  In the case where the username is not provided, and the cache contains multiple identities, even if they have the same username, the overall call would fail (the structure of this path was a little confusing to me at first, but I believe that what ends up happening is that if there are multiple entries the `IAccount` set, on the first iteration of the [loop](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-java/pull/5917/files#diff-7ccb32d40bee8ed1a674b839b685263fR84-R95), `requestedAccount` will be `null`, and so we'll set `cached` to the first element in the set.  Then on the next iteration, `requestedAccount` will be set, but one of the `homeAccountId()` (a unique identifier across tenets) or `environment()` will differ (even if the username is the same) and we will continue to fail.  In the case where there are multiple identities with the same username and a username was provided, we just use the "first" one from the cache.

Given the above, I'm tempted to revert back to the behavior I had previously, where we fail in all cases where there were multiple identities after doing our filtering.  I'm not 100% certain that our strategy of "just pick the first one that works" is actually correct, for example, if there are multiple `matell@microsoft.com`'s in the cache with different `HomeAccountIds` or `Environments`, it's not clear to me which of the identities we'd want to use there.  The behavior in this case would then align with .NET.

This change requires some explanation...

For those who aren't already familiar, the Go SDK marshaling goes through two layers:

1. First the Go SDK types are marshaled into `resource.PropertyValue` values. That's what this PR is changing.
2. Then when marshaling for gRPC calls, the `resource.PropertyValue` values are marshaled into the gRPC structures. Support for marshaling output values at this layer was already implemented in #7861.

During (2), when the option to `KeepOutputValues` isn't set, the same values as before these changes are returned.

For (1), when marshaling `Output`, the behavior of this PR is currently to always return an output value. (It's up to the `KeepOutputValues` option in (2) to determine whether the output values are kept or not when going across the gRPC boundary.)

This does lead to this unusual case for this test where we have some input values that are `StringOutput` that are being passed to `marshalInputs`, and since we're now returning `Output` as an output value, we need to change the expected values for this test.

I can imagine an alternative behavior for `marshalInputs`, where if the `Output`'s value is known, isn't a secret, and doesn't have any dependencies, we could just return the same value as before, so this test wouldn't have to change. We could similarly do that when the output is a secret and doesn't have any dependencies, just return the same value that would be returned previously. And we could even do it for unknowns when there aren't any dependencies. I don't feel strongly about this either way, as it doesn't really affect anything in practice (aside from this test), but curious what other folks think.

Yea, the skipping recursing into subdirectories based on wildcards is optimization. To the extent that it is buggy, we should bias toward correctness.

I had hoped that we would be able to use the same code here as we use for [glob matching against a filesystem](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/2fbd8bb618cbc06bb242e461be9e9942e7699e65/src/rust/engine/fs/src/glob_matching.rs#L370-L380), but the primary blocking issue for that is exactly that this code is trying to shortcircuit and skip recursing into things (i.e. "match an entire subdirectory"), and that's not an operation that we can do in the filesystem (due to symlinks, etc).

It's possible that the implementations could be merged, but in the meantime: removing some optimization to ensure that this is correct is the only course.

Not exactly, it's more like doing a `mask` on the data, that's required to be aggregated, so it's like `mask` of the raw frame's data after grouping:
<details><summary>Code</summary>

```python
>>> df
   a  b  c
0  1  5  3
1  1  4  4
2  2  3  5
>>> df[["a", "b"]].groupby("a").sum()
   b
a
1  9
2  3
>>> df.groupby("a")[["a", "b"]].sum()
   a  b
a
1  2  9
2  2  3
```

</details>

We can't achieve an effect of aggregating 'by' columns only doing a mask before groupby, unless we'll detach 'by' data from the frame:
<details><summary>Code</summary>

```python
>>> df[["a", "b"]].groupby(df["a"]).sum()
   a  b
a
1  2  9
2  2  3
```

</details>

For now, there's no convention on what backend's groupby obtains as 'by', in some cases, it's detached columns (as query compiler of the 'by' columns), in other cases, it's a list of string column names.

It's not obvious which way of passing 'by' was added by mistake and so which one should be avoided, most of our implementation implies that 'by' is a query compiler, supposing that 'by' as query compiler is the true way, however #2744 notation (as I thought this PR should show an idealistic API that we want to follow) it [forces us to always use columns names](https://github.com/RehanSD/modin/blob/d31e9640c205489716f9bcb61d61ee82e79bfb44/modin/engines/modin_dataframe.py#L257-L258) as 'by's.

To be able to work properly with both of 'by' types, we must pass extra information about actual columns that have to be aggregated (`selection` parameter).

BTW, our current groupby implementation for pandas backend always translates "detached" 'by' into the "attached" ones ([[1]](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/c4a7355e84c3eb23891555237f29c340b864a0d9/modin/backends/pandas/query_compiler.py#L2719-L2723) [[2]](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/c4a7355e84c3eb23891555237f29c340b864a0d9/modin/data_management/functions/groupby_function.py#L124-L133)) so we will always need to generate some kind of meta-info about actual selection (if we remove `selection` backend parameter, we still need this info, so it would be computed anyway inside backend's groupby).

>  the ~normalish way of setting this up is by pointing some set of environment variables at the ENVIRONMENT_DIR (and subdirs).

That's what I saw. Problem is (and that's a peculiarity of R) the most operations happen in an R session, not the terminal session. `renv` can only be activated properly from inside R. For that reason, my current suggestion is to place a `renv::activate()` call in a file, then point the `R_PROFILE_USER` environment variable to that file so the code will run at [R startup](https://rstats.wtf/r-startup.html). The idea is now to allow two versions, inline code expressions and file in `entry:`:
```bash
Rscript -e expr # like Rscript -e "1+1" 
Rscript path/to/file 
```
An inject the options `--no-save`, `--no-restore`, `--no-site-file` and `--no-environ` after `Rscript` (to basically have `--vanilla` start, but run this one `renv::activate()` statement) and expand the path similar to `language: script` since we have to run the hooks in the working directory of the repo. The files and the hook args are then passed after the above, i.e. 
```r
Rscript --no-save --no-restore --no-site-file --no-environ /abs/path/ [files] [hook args]
Rscript --no-save --no-restore --no-site-file --no-environ -e expr [files] [hook args]
```

1. Write a YAML that installs docker
2. Run said YAML with `ray up <>.yaml`

3. Edit YAML to exclude all docker install scripts
4. Wait ~10 seconds
5. Run `ray up <>.yaml`

This is an error we can no longer detect. The setup for this test is... odd. It starts from an empty base state and wants to validate that two resources cannot mention the same alias. The only way to do that from the statefile alone is to always store all of the aliases in the statefile. In order to support this check, we'd need to move the handling of that specific case into the step generator. I did this in a different branch, but I got a little bit more ambitious there and fixed a few other bugs I found along the way: https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/compare/pgavlin/choose-single-alias

IMO, this specific check is relatively low-value, as it's really only covering the case where resources that _have no base state_ cannot be mention the same alias. If the resources are both aliased to the same base state, then we will still issue an error (just added a test for that).

There is one interesting case that we don't catch in either case: given the base state `[A]`, it is possible to register a resource `A` and a resource `B` that is aliased to `A`. This is bad, and we should fix it, but that requires fixing a nasty bug in provider aliasing. That bug is an easy fix, I think (a possible fix is included in that other branch), but I'd like to separate it out nonetheless.

We had a quick offline discussion about this. The difference between these two is as follows:
- If an op has a `copy` parameter it might be an actual no-op if `copy=False`. With `copy=True` or if any operation is performed, the user gets a new tensor. The old tensor is never changed inplace.
- If an op has an `inplace` parameter, it will always perform an operation. Whether this operation is performed inplace or on a new tensor is controlled by the flag.

Since the `convert_*` kernels fall in the first category, we are keeping the `copy=True` parameter.

It's useful for when you're using this datastructure to seed an upstream search from a pixel coordinate rather than taking the step of having the projected coordinate and then inverse geotransforming it back into raster space. All the hydrological algorithms we have operate at the raster/pixel scale so that's just a handy way of getting it back down there.

A very concrete example of this is where I just used it where I needed to calculate the number of beneficiaries "downstream" from a point. You can do this by using a "distance to channel" function with a weighted raster that is the beneficiary. However you need to also provide where your channels are. Since I actually wanted it to go to the outlet I use this data structure to make a raster that only has the pixels set to 1 where these points are.

It totally does.  StreamIterators are designed to be chained, so most explicit cases would be:

```
s.flat.iter.getElementsByOffset(10.0, 20.0).getElementsByClass('Note').getElementsByGroup('cue').addFilter(lambda n: n.pitch.alter == 1)
```

As of v.6., each of these "getElementsBy..." returns a new StreamIterator that adds a new filter on the notes.  So `s.getElementsByClass(stream.Part).getElementsByClass(chord.Chord)` would always return no elements, because first it has to be of class Part, and then of class Chord, and nothing will ever be like that.

It might be that adding a second filter of the same type should raise an exception, but there are a few cases where you might want this, such as:

`s.getElementsByClass(spanner.Spanner).getElementsByClass(expressions.Tremolo)`

which only gets Tremolos that are Spanners.

Our messages crossed. In general, it's not a bad technique for the entry of a recursive call to create mutable lists and for these lists to be mutably appended through it, but your last commit was necessary to make that work, now that they're not global.

Functionally passing immutable objects down a recursive walk through a tree makes the changes apply to subtrees; non-functionally passing a mutable object and modifying it in place through the recurisve walk makes changes apply in the "reading order" through the tree. This is a walk over an AST of some code, where both cases can be relevant.

For instance, if you were walking over an AST of this code:

```python
def f(x):
    y = (x + 1)**2
    return y + 1
```

passing an immutable thing into the first Assign node, it wouldn't exist in the Return, but if you are in-place updating a list or dict, it's still available in the Return node. The immutable-passing is good for nested scopes, such as the scope of variables available in function `f`, but the mutable-passing is good for accumulating things, like which variables exist by the time you get to a particular node.

So it's not in principle bad that these variables are being changed in-place, if the latter feature is desired. Those variables do, however, have to be cleared or created anew at the beginning of recursion and always passed through all other steps of recursion. For things like this, I often separate the "entry point" of recursion with one function and the "continue walking" with another function. The "continue walking" function shouldn't have default arguments (to avoid errors like the one that Henry fixed), but the "entry point" often should, for a nice API.

For an alternative, while writing the above, my initial was:
- The base task or base salesforce task always includes "base" task options of the options needed to inject namespaces: `managed`, `namespaced_org`, and `namespace_[to_]inject`.
    - This would change how task options are compiled.   Task implementations can override the base task options, but at least the options would still exist.  Something like:
       ```python
        task_options = self.base_task_options
        task_options.update(self.task_options)
        ```
- The base task then has an instance method called `_inject_namespace` that does all the calculation.
    - At first, I was worried about breaking existing implementations, but then I remember it's python: any implementation using the same method name will just override the base method.
    - Some extra care may need to be taking since we are "overriding" task options.  I think the trick is never to have the base task reset base task options.  `_inject_namespace` always reads the task options for its calculations.  
    -  If the base task options are overridden in a way that breaks `_inject_namespace`, oops!  Don't use `_inject_namespace`.
- Tasks like `Deploy` that use non-standard task options for namespace injection can do the conversion in `_init_options`.

If your compiler doesn't support the feature, you get a nice, configure time error (more applicable to the non-meta feature method). If you have a `CXX_STANDARD` set, then that is used for the flag, or (again) a nice configure time error is given you try to set a standard too low.

I think that bool functions should start with `is` if and only if they are describing an object. For example, `pandas.Index.is_unique` is describing the index. 

```python
if index.is_unique:
  foo()
```

is "if index is unique, `foo()`", which sounds a lot like the full sentence "if the index is unique, do `foo()`".

Here `use_inferred_columns_names` is not describing something. It's telling us whether to take the action of using inferred column names." If I write 

```python
if is_use_inferred_columns_names:
  pass_names()
```

it sounds like "if is use inferred columns names, pass names." On the other hand, if we had `use_inferred_columns_names`, the code would sound like "If use inferred columns names, pass names." That sounds much more natural to me.

If you find this crazy _early and _late cache key business hard to understand, I can explain in more detail (probably makes sense to add comments to these functions but wanted to see if you ended up wanting to simplify it first).

This approach was necessary to get `Model[List[T]][int]` to be the same type as `Model[List[int]]`. It could be modified to not use two different cache keys at different points, but the `_early_cache_key` is optimized to be as quick to compute as possible (for repeated-access speed), and the `_late_cache_key` is optimized to be as "correct" as possible, so that two types that are ultimately the same after resolving the type arguments will always produce cache hits.

Hm ... but what if the error indicator is set (for whatever reason)? We're leaving the process in an invalid state, follow-on Python C API calls may behave in confusing ways (painful debugging memories come back).

If the Python documentation guarantees that the error indicator is not set unless `destructor == nullptr`, this change is fine.

Looking: https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/capsule.html#c.PyCapsule_GetContext
________ 
Return the current context stored in the capsule. On failure, set an exception and return NULL.

It is legal for a capsule to have a NULL context. This makes a NULL return code somewhat ambiguous; use [PyCapsule_IsValid()](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/capsule.html#c.PyCapsule_IsValid) or [PyErr_Occurred()](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_Occurred) to disambiguate.
________

To me it sounds like:

* If the error indicator is set the return value is certainly nullptr.
* But knowing that the return value is nullptr does not tell us anything about the state of the error indicator.

So checking the error indicator is mandatory, unless we want to risk leaving the process in an invalid state, which I don't think is a responsible thing to do, even it this was more than a minor optimization.

actually, the meat of the algorithm is the block following the ones you referenced.

The big picture is that this piece of code (re-)implements 2d convolution, but with a twist to enable the rolling ball algorithm. Here, I'm implementing the version that interprets convolution as matrix multiplications. ([This blog post](https://sgugger.github.io/convolution-in-depth.html) provides a nice overview of that idea.)

For this, we need to identify the indices of pixels affected by a single application of the kernel. Given an anchor for the kernel (top-left corner), the first block of code computes those indices relative to the anchor. The second block of code then computes all anchor points where the kernel must be applied.

The third block (that you didn't reference) combines each anchor with the relative window coordinates (`filter_idx = anchor_offsets[...`), extracts the affected image intensities, and applies the kernel (by multiplying with it). At this point, there are 3 major differences from standard convolution to make the rolling ball method work (which prevent us from using an existing conv2d implementation):

1. Instead of just doing `px_value * kernel` we have to add the value of the sagitta to each intensity value first `(px_value + sagitta) * kernel`. This is the only difference between `rolling_ball` and the `rolling_disk` approximation.
2. We don't reduce the vector using a sum (like in standard convolution), but, instead, use min.
3. The kernel is large (`>>(50,50)`), so we can't fit the entire `px_value` matrix into memory; instead we have to do it in batches.

# models.py
class MyPeriodicTask(PeriodicTask):
    other_model = models.ForeignKey(
        'OtherModel',
        null=True,
        on_delete=models.CASCADE
    )
    def save(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.kwargs = json.dumps('a_field_key',self.other_model.a_field)
        super().save(*args, **kwargs)
        # The bug is in it: 
        # This `save()` will call the `save_base()`.
        # But in `save_base()`, it will set `pre_save.sender = MyPeriodicTask`, not `PeriodicTask`.
        # So, the `PeriodicTask` can not receive signal with

I am relying on the webserver to set the headers correctly. -- which is a nightmare. 
- Github(or random webserver with publicly accessible space) does not include content-type nor content-disposition so I extract the file extension from the URL
- Dropbox sets content-type to application/binary for gzip but includes the content-disposition so I parse the content-disposition for the filename
- Google includes content-type as well as content-disposition

I am actually not sure what to do here now. Google is the only one you can't determine the compression type from the file extension at the end of the URL but it seems dicey and brittle to rely on the URL to tell you the compression type anyway. 

Another option would be to read the first few bytes of the file and map that to the compression type -- but that also seems likely to produce a false positive at some point:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19120676/how-to-detect-type-of-compression-used-on-the-file-if-no-file-extension-is-spe

We could also just let the shutil.unpack_archive function give all the various compression types a shot until it works -- zip, gztar, etc. Something like this:
try:
    shutil.unpack_archive(temp_download_file, "/tmp", "zip")
except Exception:
    shutil.unpack_archive(temp_download_file, "/tmp", "gztar")

And finally, I guess we could force the user to include the compression type on the link puller URL but that seems like a hassle.

And I'm a bit confused by the two similar-looking constants!

We have `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CSL_RECEIVE_TIME_AHEAD` which is the time between telling the radio to go into receive-mode (at a particular scheduled time, or asap - depending on the platform) and when it's ready to receive, worst-case.

I'm assuming that after this time period the radio is for sure ready to receive a frame correctly and the preamble can arrive.

So the time t at which the radio needs to be "scheduled to receive on" is :

`t = cslTimeMHR - 192 - OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CSL_RECEIVE_TIME_AHEAD`

in which case we don't use the other constant `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MIN_RECEIVE_ON_AHEAD`.

Or it could be this time:

`t = cslTimeMHR - OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CSL_RECEIVE_TIME_AHEAD` in which case we also don't use the other constant `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MIN_RECEIVE_ON_AHEAD`.

Or it could be this time:

`t = cslTimeMHR - OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MIN_RECEIVE_ON_AHEAD - OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CSL_RECEIVE_TIME_AHEAD` 

in which case we use both constants, however it's not clear from the `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CSL_RECEIVE_TIME_AHEAD` definition that it means this. And `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MIN_RECEIVE_ON_AHEAD`  would always be 192 us because that's determined by the standard and not by the particular platform so it shouldn't be in the platform config then.

Given that JVM selection options are still very much in flux in the current JVM backends, I propose to punt this problem to later when we have a better idea of how target-level JDK constraints are going to interact with subsystem and rule level JVM selection.  If we add an explicit `jdk-use-system` later, it's easy to make `jdk=system` an alias for that without breaking any workflows; whereas splitting the option out now starts to constrain our interface in ways we might want to change in the near future.

Some initial thoughts:

- Some of the advice, like running `certbot renew --pre-hook ...` is not up-to-date with the practices that we would suggest to users today. Especially for snap users, they cannot modify their scheduled task like that. Things should be done on a per-certificate basis using flags. (Or by using the `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks`, but those have a confusing caveat which makes me shy away from ever recommending that approach). `certbot reconfigure`, once merged, is relevant here too.
- We should cover the use of `--pre-hook`, `--post-hook`, `--deploy-hook`, on certificate creation, not at renewal time. That feels like the relevant time to learn about them. This could be a note in the "Getting certificates" section linking to a later section.

If you're planning on converting all `rpad_and_clip(target, axis)` into `rpad(target, axis, clip=True)`, in preparation for the name change (turning function names into argument values is more awkward than just changing names), know that there's an easier way to do it than doing it all by hand.

In Emacs, this is an ideal candidate for a keyboard macro:

```lisp
M-x start-kbd-macro            ; C-x (
M-x search-forward             ; C-s
.rpad_and_clip(
M-x backward-char              ; C-b or left arrow
M-x delete-backward-char × 9   ; backspace 9 times to delete "_and_clip"
M-x forward-sexp               ; C-M-f to go forward over parenthesized expression
M-x backward-char              ; C-b or left arrow
, clip=True
M-x end-kbd-macro              ; C-x )
```

Then you can run the above keyboard macro by `M-x call-last-kbd-macro` or `C-x e` (and repeatedly by pressing `e` thereafter). It would search forward to all ".rpad_and_clip", erase the "_and_clip", jump to the end of the parenthesized expression (regardless of whatever's in it), and add the ", clip=True". It's the sort of thing that I would never do through `M-x` commands—I'd use the keyboard shortcuts; I'm just being explicit to be understood. Since some of those shortcuts navigate syntactically through the code (jumping over parentheses), it's possible to do sophisticated, syntax-aware edits, not just find-and-replace. Since it plays them back only once per `C-x e` (or `e`), you get to keep an eye on it and make sure that it's not messing things up—semi-automated can be overall faster than manual _and_ faster than full automation, since a fully automated script can fail to handle unforeseen cases and then you have to go back and fix the mess it's made.

I'm telling you all of this so that we globally optimize our use of time. If you would have done this edit manually or by writing a script, note that it can be done in less than a minute with a keyboard macro. Either I'll do it later, as one of the tasks in a future renaming pass, or you might want to try it in Emacs, or—best yet—you can tell me if a feature like this exists in any modern (i.e. non-Emacs) IDE. I'd love to know. I've been searching for it, wondering how users of modern IDEs get along without having this feature, but maybe it exists by another name.

> text from Type Rules by Operator section

Good reference, although I think it's more related to actual dataframe algebra defined in a low-level dataframe object. Our "algebra" module is more like wrappers around actual operators, thus I think it's acceptable here to pass instructions on how to get new dtypes instead of already computed ones.

Regarding where to put the inferring logic: I believe it would be better to encapsulate it in the Binary operator. Since the logic tends to be only binary ops related it's then unnecessary to bloat general QC with operator-specific functionality.

As for configuring the type-inferring logic, I would propose something like this. A parameter for `.register` that either takes a resulted type (bool for logical ops, float for `truediv`, ...) or an "infer" keyword, indicating automatic type inferring.
```python
dtypes : {int, float, bool, object, 'infer', None}, default: None
    Specifies a dtype for the result of the binary operation. If 'infer' - try to
    automatically infer the resulted type based on the operand's types. If ``None``
    - do not infer new dtypes (they will be computed manually once accessed).
```
If you don't like mixing actual types and 'infer' in a single parameter we could either reformat the parameter a bit:
```python
how_compute_dtypes : enum.HOW_INFER_TYPE = {INT, FLOAT, BOOL, NOHOW, AUTO}
```
or split them into two separate parameters:
```python
def register(..., dtypes=None, auto_dtypes_inferring=False): # 'dtypes' is ignored if 'auto_dtypes_inferring=True' was specified
```

I have a concern: if users call checker with full_check, did they expect the ONNX model will get updated (shape_inference will bring inferred shapes in value_info)?

Current behavior:
- check_model+full_check with model path (Python and C++ API): the original model will be overrode by the inferred model thru model path (it's by default from onnx.shape_inference_path)
- check_model+full_check with ModelProto (Python and C++ API): the original model won't be updated

After this PR:
- check_model+full_check with model path (Python and C++ API): same as current behavior
- check_model+full_check with ModelProto (Python API): the original model won't be updated
- check_model+full_check with ModelProto (C++ API): the original ModelProto will be overrode by the inferred model

IMO, ideally all APIs behaviors for check_model should be consistent to prevent confusion. It seems weird to me that the ONNX model will be updated after onnx.checker with full_check. Still, it's an existing issue (check_model does not update the model, but check_model_path does) so it should not be the blocker to this PR. If both of you also think it's a valid concern, probably we can improve it in another PR.

I was thinking about the different between config-based / object-based approach here.
I do not have a recommendation, as neither is clear winner, but I came to think that the major difference is that accepting only config means that the code is allowing only a set of encoders parameterized by the config, while accepting an object means that the code is allowing any encoder with the same interface (duck-typing).

FYI: normally it's frowned upon to use `Path` instead of the engine's types instead of Pants's filesystem intrinsics like `PathGlobs`. That's important to do so that Pants's file watching invalidation works properly.

But I think it's 100% necessary to do here because these file paths are ignored by Pants's file watcher, and it's also not currently even possible for the engine to read in these files (they're under `pants_ignore` normally). 

This should be safe from a cache perspective because `@goal_rule`s are not cached/memoized. It would not be safe if you were calling these methods in a normal `@rule` though.
